rulings.Case Title: PROTUS TANIFORM VS. COMMISSION ON TEACHER CREDENTIALLING, AN AGENCY
rulings.Calendar Matter: Order To Show Cause (Per Ex Parte Order Filed 3/15/19)
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON WEDNESDAY, APRIL 3, 2019, LINE 3 Petitioner Protus Taniform's application to stay revocation of teaching credentials is DENIED. Petitioner has not shown that Respondent California Commission on Teacher Credentialing is unlikely to prevail on the merits. (Code Civ. Proc. § 1094.5(h)(1).) Petitioner has not shown that the administrative law judge committed a prejudicial abuse of discretion in applying the Morrison factors (Morrison v. State Board of Education (1969) 1 Cal.3d 214, 229; 5 Cal. Code Regs. § 80302) or that the ALJ's findings regarding those factors are not supported by the weight of the evidence. (See Broney v. California Comm'n on Teacher Credentialing (2010) 184 Cal.App.4th 462, 476-479.) Because Petitioner has failed to establish the agency is unlikely to prevail, the Court need not consider the public interest criterion. (See Medical Board of California v. Superior Court (1991) 227 Cal.App.3d 1458, 1461 [trial court abused its discretion in staying administrative order revoking petitioner's license to practice as physician].) rulings.Case Title: JANE DOE VS. KAISER FOUNDATION HOSPITALS DBA KAISER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON WEDNESDAY, APRIL 3, 2019, LINE 4 DEFENDANT THE PERMANENTE MEDICAL GROUP, INC.'s Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings The hearing will be at 9am, not 9:30am. Defendant The Permanente Medical Group. Inc.'s motion for judgment on the pleadings is denied. TPMG's standing argument is no longer tenable in light of plaintiff Jane Doe's application to reopen her chapter 7 bankruptcy case; the decision by the appointed bankruptcy trustee for that case not to pursue Ms. Doe's litigation claims against TPMG; and thereafter the bankruptcy court's order closing the reopened case. At the hearing on March 12, 2019 counsel for TPMG stated that TPMG had three different arguments why its motion should be granted based on judicial estoppel or related grounds. Those arguments are that Ms. Doe's claims fail as a matter of law because: 1) the initial chapter 7 bankruptcy papers that were filed in 2014 did not make any reference to Ms. Doe's claims against TPMG; 2) the papers filed in 2018 to reopen the chapter 7 bankruptcy case did not refer to this litigation but only to an EEOC charge that Ms. Doe filed against TPMG; and 3) the papers filed in 2018 to reopen the chapter 7 bankruptcy case state that Ms. Doe did not believe a bankruptcy trustee should be appointed. On this motion for judgment on the pleadings all three of TPMG's arguments lack merit. (Cloud v. Northrop Grumman Corp. (1998) 67 Cal. App. 4th 995, 1011-1021). Per Cloud, which remains good law and has been cited approvingly by numerous subsequent published California decisions, the failure to include a litigation claim other than a claim against a creditor in the bankruptcy case in papers with the bankruptcy court is not grounds to preclude the litigation claim absent evidence that the bankruptcy debtor engaged in a deliberate scheme to mislead and gain an unfair advantage. Since this is a non-evidentiary motion, the requisite factual findings can't be made on this motion. While the precise holding of Cloud defeats TPMG's first argument, the reasoning of Cloud also makes clear that TPMG's second and third arguments are not amenable to resolution in TPMG's favor on this motion. There is no basis on this motion to find that Ms. Doe's 2018 bankruptcy papers failed to refer to this litigation or stated that a bankruptcy trustee should not be appointed because of any effort on Ms. Doe's part to mislead the bankruptcy court or bankruptcy trustee and to gain an unfair advantage. Moreover, judicial estoppel could not apply to Ms. Doe's statement that a bankruptcy trustee should not be appointed because Ms. Doe was not successful in her apparent effort not to have a bankruptcy trustee appointed in her reopened chapter 7 case. (See Gottlieb v. Kest (2006) 141 Cal. App. 4th 110, 137-38 (judicial estoppel does not apply to a position taken by bankruptcy debtor in the bankruptcy case unless the bankruptcy debtor was successful in asserting that position in the bankruptcy case)). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms. Doe is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. = EPS

rulings.Case Title: ROBERT GRISSOM VS. WESTAR MARIN SERVICES ET AL
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON WEDNESDAY, APRIL 3, 2019, LINE 5, Motion 2 of 2 CROSS DEFENDANT A. JOSEPH SALAIS, PH.D.'S DEMURRER TO the cross-complaint filed by defendant and cross-complainant Crosslink, Inc. dba Westar Marine Services OVERRULED. Westar has sufficiently pled facts supporting its claims for indemnity and contribution based on the alleged fault of Dr. Salais in causing plaintiff Grissom's injuries. Although the pleadings do not allege what specific acts by Dr. Salais constitute a breach of the applicable standard of care, general allegations in a medical malpractice cause of action are sufficient to withstand a general demurrer. (See Landeros v. Flood (1976) 17 Cal.3d 399, 407 ["'In this state negligence may be pleaded in general terms, and that is as true of malpractice cases as it is of other types of negligence cases.'"].) "Negligence may be alleged in general terms; that is, it is sufficient to allege an act was negligently done without stating the particular omission which rendered it negligent." (Hahn v. Mirda (2007) 147 Cal.App.4th 740, 746-747 [allegations that defendant doctors "negligently cared for, diagnosed and treated" plaintiff were sufficient].) The cross-complaint sufficiently alleges that Dr. Salais, a psychologist, undertook the diagnosis, care and treatment of plaintiff on or about May 18, 2017 and that he failed to exercise proper degree of care and skills and performed unnecessary and negligent treatment resulting in personal injuries (Cross-Complaint 2, 11, 12). The cross-complaint is not subject to a special demurrer because it adequately puts Dr. Salais on notice of the claims against him to enable him to file an answer. "A demurrer for uncertainty is strictly construed, even where a complaint is in some respects uncertain, because ambiguities can be clarified under modern discovery procedures," especially when the particular facts are within the presumptive knowledge of the defendant, as in the case at hand. (Khoury v. Maly's of California, Inc. (1993) 14 Cal.App.4th 612, 616.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Westar is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. = EPS

rulings.Case Title: ROBERT GRISSOM VS. WESTAR MARIN SERVICES ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Crosslink, Inc. Dba Westar Marine Services Cross-Complaint
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON WEDNESDAY, APRIL 3, 2019, LINE 5, Motion 1 of 2 CROSS DEFENDANT A. JOSEPH SALAIS, PH.D.'s Motion To Strike Crosslink, Inc. Dba Westar Marine Services Cross-Complaint DENIED and the motion to strike paragraphs praying for attorney fees and associated costs is GRANTED with 20 days leave to amend. The Court treats Westar's opposition to Dr. Salais's motion to strike as the equivalent of a motion for leave to file a cross-complaint pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 428.50(c) and grants leave per the stipulation between Westar and plaintiff Robert Grissom. The cross-complaint does not allege any statutory or contractual basis for its prayer for attorney fees and costs, nor does it allege facts supporting the application of the tort of another doctrine. The tort of another doctrine applies when one "through the tort of another has been required to act in the protection of his interests by bringing or defending an action against a third person." (Lederer v. Gursey Schneider LLP (2018) 22 Cal.App.5th 508, 527.) The rule has been strictly applied and "nearly all of the cases which have applied the [Prentice v. North Amer. Title Guar. Corp. (1963) 59 Cal.2d 618] doctrine involve a clear violation of a traditional tort duty between the tortfeasor who is required to pay the attorney fees and the person seeking compensation for those fees. . . The rule of Prentice was not intended to apply to one of several joint tortfeasors in order to justify additional attorney fee damages." (David v. Hermann (2005) 129 Cal.App.4th 672, 688-689.) The cross-complaint does not allege any duty owed by the alleged indemnitor to the indemnitee. Furthermore, the cross-complaint does not allege that Westar had to defend a lawsuit because of Dr. Salais' tortious conduct; rather, Westar has to defend because plaintiff sustained an industrial injury. (See UMET Trust v. Santa Monica Medical Investment Co. (1983) 140 Cal.App.3d 864, 866 ["The exceptional circumstances doctrine is not applied in cases where the party seeking reimbursement for counsel fees is not compelled or required to bring or defend an action against the third person."]). Westar is given leave to amend if it can allege in good faith facts supporting a prayer for attorney's fees and associated costs. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Dr. Salais is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. = EPS

rulings.Case Title: AIMEE BAINTER VS. BENEFIT COSMETICS, LLC
rulings.Calendar Matter: Part 2 Plaintiffs' Motion For Approval Of Private Attorneys General Act (Cal. Labor Code 2698, Et Seq.) Settlement Agreement And Award Of Attorneys' Fees And Costs, Incentive Payments, And Administrator Costs
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON WEDNESDAY, APRIL 3, 2019, LINE 8, Part 2 of 2 Fourth, Plaintiffs have provided the Court with insufficient information to evaluate the reasonableness of the award of attorneys' fees they seek under the lodestar method, which the Court intends to use as a cross-check against the percentage method. The Attorney Task and Time Chart attached as Exhibit 2 to the Aiwazian Declaration reveals the number of hours devoted by Plaintiffs' counsel to the listed tasks, but does not disclose the names or experience of the individual attorneys who performed those tasks. Nor do the moving papers provide an adequate basis for concluding that $550 per hour is a reasonable overall hourly rate. Paragraph 26 of the Aiwazian Declaration discloses only the average hourly rate awarded by other courts to Plaintiffs' counsel in other cases. Without more detailed information, the Court cannot determine a reasonable lodestar. Fifth, Plaintiffs have not provided any information regarding the experience or qualifications of Simpluris, Inc., the proposed Settlement Administrator. Sixth, Paragraph III(S)(12) of the Settlement Agreement appears to contain an incomplete sentence ["To the extent that any Party, including any PAGA Group Member, commences any legal action or other proceeding against any other Party to enforce the provisions of this Agreement, to declare rights and/or obligations under this Agreement, or to enjoin any proceedings in violation of this Agreement."]. To the extent that the parties intended that paragraph to contain a prevailing party attorneys' fees clause, the Court will not approve such a clause as to actions or proceedings brought by PAGA Group Members. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. = EPS

rulings.Case Title: AIMEE BAINTER VS. BENEFIT COSMETICS, LLC
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs' Notice Of Motion And Motion For Approval Of Private Attorneys General Act (Cal. Labor Code 2698, Et Seq.) Settlement Agreement And Award Of Attorneys' Fees And Costs, Incentive Payments, And Administrator Costs; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON WEDNESDAY, APRIL 3, 2019, LINE 8, Part 1 of 2 PLAINTIFFS AIMEE BAINTER and NATALIE MERCHANT'S Motion For Approval Of Private Attorneys General Act (Cal. Labor Code 2698, Et Seq.) Settlement Agreement And Award Of Attorneys' Fees And Costs, Incentive Payments, And Administrator Costs DENIED without prejudice to a renewed motion following appropriate amendments to the proposed settlement agreement and/or a further showing as to the following issues. First, while the Total Settlement Amount of $1.7 million is undoubtedly substantial, Plaintiffs have not provided the Court with any information regarding Defendant's maximum total exposure to civil penalties, if Plaintiffs were to prevail at trial. The Court acknowledges that, like any settlement, the Settlement Agreement reflects a compromise of disputed claims. However, the Court does not believe it can adequately discharge its duty to "ensur[e] that any negotiated resolution is fair to those affected" (Williams v. Superior Court (2017) 3 Cal.5th 531, 549, citing Lab. Code § 2699(l)(2)) without ascertaining the relationship between the Total Settlement Amount and the maximum potential recovery calculated by Plaintiffs or their damages expert, based upon the substantial number of PAGA Group Members (approximately 1,175) and the lengthy Covered Period (June 20, 2016 through the date the Court enters the Settlement Approval Order). Second, Paragraph III(A) of the Settlement Agreement provides that Plaintiffs will stipulate to the dismissal with prejudice of the Action in its entirety. The Action will not be dismissed; rather, the Court will enter judgment, as contemplated in Exhibit A to the Settlement Agreement. Third, the proposed release in Paragraphs III(I)(1) and III(N) of the Settlement Agreement appears overly broad. Neither the LWDA nor PAGA Group Members are parties to this representative action, and they therefore cannot be deemed to have released any claims. The judgment in the action will be binding on the named Plaintiffs, on government agencies and any aggrieved employee not a party to the proceeding. (Arias v. Superior Court (2009) 46 Cal.4th 969, 985-986.) The Court will not approve the language in Paragraph III(N) providing that no PAGA Group Member will have any right to object to the Settlement. Nor will it approve the language in the proposed cover letter to PAGA Group Members (Exhibit B to the Settlement Agreement) indicating that the settlement resolves "any and all claims or causes of action for penalties under PAGA, whether known or unknown . . . ." Continues in Part 2 of 2.

rulings.Case Title: HOWARD JARVIS VS. THE BAY AREA TOLL AUTHORITY
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Bay Area Toll Authority'S Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON WEDNESDAY, APRIL 3, 2019, LINE 10, Motion 1 of 2 DEFENDANT BAY AREA TOLL AUTHORITY'S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings GRANTED without leave to amend as to the second cause of action for declaratory relief and invalidation of Regional Measure 3. Article XIIIC, section 1 of the California Constitution applies only to taxes imposed by local governments. Because Defendant California State Legislature imposed the challenged toll increase by passing SB 595 and because BATA is a regional entity charged with implementing that state mandate, Article XIIIA, section 3 of the Constitution applies to this state-imposed charge, not Article XIIIC, section 1. (Compare Cal. Const. art. XIIIA, § 3(b) ["As used in this section, 'tax' means any levy, charge, or exaction of any kind imposed by the State" with five exceptions] [emphasis added] with Cal. Const. art. XIIIC, § 1(e) ["As used in this article, 'tax' means any levy, charge, or exaction of any kind imposed by a local government" with seven exceptions] [emphasis added].) The State, through the Legislature, imposes tolls on state-owned toll bridges, such as those affected by the toll increase at issue here. (See Sts. & Hy. Code § 30916 [mandating base toll rate and subsequent increases in 2004 and 2019].) BATA is charged with managing and overseeing the collection of toll revenues that derive from tolls imposed by the Legislature. (Id. §§ 30886, 30950, 30950.2.) Plaintiffs' allegation in its first amended complaint that the Legislature merely authorized BATA to impose the toll increase is not a factual allegation that must be accepted as true. Rather, it is a legal conclusion that is afforded no deference on a motion for judgment on the pleadings. (See Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318 [on a demurrer or motion for judgment on the pleadings, all properly pled ultimate facts are treated as true, "but not contentions, deductions or conclusions of fact or law."].) While BATA had discretion to determine the amount of the toll increase (up to $3.00), it had to implement an increase as directed by the Legislature. (See Sts. & Hy. Code § 30916(c)(1) ["[BATA] shall increase the base toll rate for vehicles crossing the bridges . . . by the amount approved by the voters pursuant to Section 30923."] [emphasis added].) Likewise, BATA had discretion to decide in which election the vote on Regional Measure 3 would be held, but it had to select one and put the measure on the ballot. (See Sts. & Hy. Code §§ 30923(c) [stating that each of the affected county boards of supervisors "shall call a special election" that "shall be consolidated within a statewide primary or general election, which shall be selected by [BATA]" and stating that BATA "shall determine the ballot question" for Regional Measure 3], 30923(d) [stating that the ballot pamphlet for the election "shall include a summary of the Regional Measure 3 expenditure plan"].) Plaintiffs provide no authority that BATA and other local entities could simply have ignored mandates of the Legislature. Finally, SB 595 and Regional Measure 3 involve a process distinct from the authorize-to-impose statutory schemes pointed to by Plaintiffs. Revenue and Taxation Code Section 7285 provides a county "may" increase the sales tax. Vehicle Code Section 9250.19 gives counties the right, but not the duty, to adopt resolutions approving increases in vehicle registration fees. Plaintiffs' reliance on Rider v. County of San Diego (1991) 1 Cal.4th 1 is misplaced because it involved a separate constitutional provision and the case was decided before the passage of Proposition 26. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. = EPS

rulings.Case Title: HOWARD JARVIS VS. THE BAY AREA TOLL AUTHORITY
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant California State Legislature'S Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment Of The Pleadings
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON WEDNESDAY, APRIL 3, 2019, LINE 10, Motion 2, Part 1 of 2 DEFENDANT CALIFORNIA STATE LEGISLATURE'S Motion For Judgment Of The Pleadings GRANTED without leave to amend as to the first cause of action for declaratory relief and invalidation of SB 595. The Legislature has met its burden to show the applicability of the exception for "entrance to or use of state property" from the general definition of "tax" in Article XIIIA, section 3(b)(4) of the California Constitution. Therefore, the toll increase imposed by SB 595 is not a tax subject to a two-thirds supermajority vote requirement. The Court takes judicial notice of the documents provided by the Legislature and by Co-Defendant Bay Area Toll Authority. The reasonable cost requirement in Article XIIIA, section 3(d) is inapplicable. In section 3(b), only the first three exceptions to the definition of "tax" contain language mandating that charges not exceed the "reasonable costs" to the State of conferring benefits or granting privileges, providing services, or performing regulatory acts. (Cal. Const. art. XIIIA, §§ 3(b)(1), 3(b)(2), 3(b)(3).) In contrast, the remaining two exceptions contain no comparable language. (Cal. Const. art. XIIIA, §§ 3(b)(4), (3)(b)(5).) Where no ambiguity exists, the language of statutes and voter initiatives amending the constitution are given their plain meaning. (Code Civ. Proc. § 1858; Schmeer v. County of Los Angeles (2013) 213 Cal.App.4th 1310, 1316 [holding that the language of the initiative is the best indicator of the voters' intent and that those words are given their ordinary and usual meaning]; see also Lesher Communications, Inc. v. City of Walnut Creek (1990) 52 Cal.3d 531, 543 ["Absent ambiguity, we presume that the voters intend the meaning apparent on the face of an initiative measure . . . and the court may not add to the statute or rewrite it to conform to an assumed intent that is not apparent in its language."] [citations omitted].) Consequently, there is no need to rely upon Plaintiffs' interpretation of voter intent in evaluating the plain language of the provision. There is no reasonableness requirement in the "charge imposed for entrance to or use of state property" exception, so it is improper to read one into the provision. (Cal. Const. art. XIIIA, § 3(b)(4).) Canons of statutory interpretation support this interpretation of the language. Reading the burden shifting language regarding reasonableness in section 3(d) as applying to all five exceptions to the definition of tax, as requested by Plaintiffs, would render references to reasonableness in the first three exceptions mere surplusage-a result to be avoided in interpreting statutes and constitutional provisions. (See People v. Hudson (2006) 38 Cal.4th 1002, 1010 ["As we have stressed in the past, interpretations that render statutory terms meaningless as surplusage are to be avoided."].) Further, the principle of avoiding absurdity in constitutional construction cautions against reading the reasonable cost requirement into the final two exceptions for charges, purchases, rentals, or leases related to state property and for fines and monetary penalties. (See City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608, 616 [holding that courts follow plain meaning "unless a literal interpretation would result in absurd consequences the Legislature did not intend"]; Amador Valley Joint Union High School District v. State Board of Equalization (1978) 22 Cal.3d 208, 245 ["The literal language of enactments may be disregarded to avoid absurd results and to fulfill the apparent intent of the framers."].) Continues in Part 2 of 2

rulings.Case Title: HOWARD JARVIS VS. THE BAY AREA TOLL AUTHORITY
rulings.Calendar Matter: Part 2, Defendant California State Legislature'S Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment Of The Pleadings
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON WEDNESDAY, APRIL 3, 2019, LINE 10, Motion 2, Part 2 of 2 Plaintiffs' contention that the reasonableness requirement should apply to half of section 3(b)(4) but not the other half is contrary to the rules of statutory construction which require, wherever reasonable, "interpretations which produce internal harmony, avoid redundancy, and accord significance to each word and phrase." (Pacific Legal Foundation v. California Unemployment Insurance Appeals Board (1981) 29 Cal.3d 101, 114.) Further, the canon of constitutional avoidance requires the Court to presume the validity of a challenged legislative act (in this case SB 595) unless the conflict with the constitution is clear and unquestionable. (See Taxpayers for Improving Public Safety v. Schwarzenegger (2009) 172 Cal.App.4th 749, 769-70 ["In considering the constitutionality of a legislative act we presume its validity, resolving all doubts in favor of the Act. Unless conflict with a provision of the state or federal Constitution is clear and unquestionable, we must uphold the Act."] [quotation omitted].) Plaintiffs' other arguments are also unpersuasive. The comparison of the toll increase to "traditional" taxes and fees is irrelevant. Proposition 26 supplanted the old "tax versus fee test," which had become complicated and unclear. (See Schmeer v. County of Los Angeles (2013) 213 Cal.App.4th 1310, 1321-22 [explaining that Proposition 26 was passed in response to the blurred distinction between fees and taxes to create broad definition of tax with specific exceptions].) Now every state-imposed levy, charge, or exaction is a tax unless it falls within one of the five express exceptions to the definition of tax. (Id.) Whether the toll increase at issue was lawfully approved must be determined under Article XIIIA, section 3 as that provision was amended by Proposition 26. The traditional distinctions between what constituted a tax and a fee do not determine whether this toll increase was lawfully approved. Prior legal requirements for fees similarly have no bearing on how the Court should evaluate the California Constitution as amended. Accordingly, the tax-versus-fee case law relied upon by Plaintiffs is outdated and uninformative in interpreting the new constitutional language. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Legislature is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. = EPS

rulings.Case Title: GUIYONG PARK VS. CRISTIAN HANCILA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service Of Summons And Complaint Or, In The Alternative, To Set Aside Request For Entry Of Default And Default
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON WEDNESDAY, APRIL 3, 2019, LINE 11 DEFENDANT CRISTIAN HANCILA'S Motion To Quash Service Of Summons And Complaint Or, In The Alternative, To Set Aside Request For Entry Of Default And Default Defendant Cristian Hancila's motion to quash service of summons is denied and his alternative motion to set aside entry of default per Code of Civil Procedure § 473 (b) is granted. Service of process was in substantial compliance with the statutory requirement for substitute service on an individual defendant under Code of Civil Procedure § 415.20 (b). (Pasadena Medi-Center Associates v. Superior Court (1973) 9 Cal.3d 773, 778 ["[s]ervice of process statutes should be 'liberally construed to effectuate service and uphold the jurisdiction of the court if actual notice has been received by the defendant']; Rodriguez v. Cho (2015) 236 Cal.App.4th 742, 750 [substitute service on office manager proper after three unsuccessful attempts to serve defendant at his place of business]; Bein v. Brechtel-Jochim Group, Inc. (1992) 6 Cal.App.4th 1387, 1393 [substitute service on security guard for gated community where defendants resided was proper].) The default is set aside pursuant to § 473(b) because defendant's attorney has filed an affidavit attesting that the default was entered because of the attorney's mistaken belief that no service had been effected and no entry of default was being sought by plaintiff before defendant attempted to file his answer two days after the entry of default. Defendant shall refile his stricken answer within five days of the entry of this order. (Carmel, Ltd. v. Tavoussi (2009) 175 Cal.App.4th 393, 401-402.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and a proposed judgment which recites all the terms of relief made in the arbitration award and must bring them to the hearing or email them to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. = EPS
